content.text: Every supervised model faces the same fundamental tension: learn the training data faithfully enough to capture real patterns, but not so faithfully that it memorises noise. Failing on the first count produces underfitting; failing on the second produces overfitting. Understanding both failure modes — and their underlying cause in the bias-variance trade-off — is the single most practical skill in applied machine learning and is directly relevant to fine-tuning the models that power agentic AI systems.
What it is

A supervised model is trained by minimising a loss function over a fixed training set. The model's parameters adjust until they minimise training loss, but the ultimate objective is to generalise — to produce correct predictions on inputs the model has never seen. Two structural failure modes prevent generalisation.
Overfitting

occurs when a model learns the training set too precisely. Because the training set is a finite, noisy sample from the true distribution, any model expressive enough to fit it perfectly will fit the noise alongside the signal. The result is low training loss but high validation and test loss — the model has memorised rather than learned.
Underfitting

occurs when the model is not expressive enough to capture the underlying relationship between inputs and outputs. A linear model applied to non-linear data, or a model trained for too few epochs on a rich dataset, will show high error on both training and held-out sets.

These are not merely theoretical curiosities. Every practical modelling workflow involves diagnosing which failure mode is active and applying the corresponding remedy. Neither is an edge case; both appear routinely whenever a model is trained on real data.
Why it matters

Production models that suffer from overfitting or underfitting degrade silently. A 98% training accuracy can mask a model that misroutes half of real user queries — because the test distribution differs even slightly from the training distribution. In agentic systems, a fine-tuned intent classifier or a retrieval ranker that overfits will behave well in offline evaluation but fail at runtime on inputs it has not seen.

LLM fine-tuning introduces a particularly acute form of overfitting: when a base model is fine-tuned on a small supervised dataset, it may reproduce training examples nearly verbatim instead of generalising the instruction format. The model passes held-in evaluation but fails on novel prompts. Practitioners who run only training-set evaluations will never detect this.

Underfitting is equally costly. An underfitted model that defaults to a constant output regardless of the input actively harms the pipeline — yet if training runs are short and evaluation is sloppy, underfitting can masquerade as a working model. The gap between training and validation metrics is the primary observable that distinguishes both failure modes from each other.
Benefits
Diagnosing overfitting and underfitting through the lens of the bias-variance trade-off gives practitioners a principled toolkit instead of ad-hoc guessing:
Predictable diagnostics
— comparing training versus validation loss immediately identifies which failure mode is active, removing ambiguity about where to invest improvement effort.
Efficient iteration
— knowing the root cause prevents wasted compute. Collecting more data fixes overfitting; adding capacity fixes underfitting. Confusing the two wastes time and money.
Better generalisation
— a model that avoids both failure modes will transfer reliably to production traffic, reducing the gap between offline metrics and live performance.
Resource efficiency
— early stopping based on validation loss avoids training beyond the optimal point, saving GPU hours on long fine-tuning runs.
Safer fine-tuning
— explicit overfitting controls (learning rate, regularisation, epoch budget) make LLM fine-tuning more predictable and less likely to degrade the base model's general capabilities.
Trade-offs
There is no single model complexity that universally avoids both failure modes — the optimal point depends on the amount and quality of available data.
Data quantity matters
— with more data, a more complex model can be used safely because individual noise points have less influence. With little data, even moderately complex models overfit.
Regularisation has a cost
— adding L1/L2 penalties or dropout reduces overfitting but can introduce or worsen underfitting if the strength is too high. Hyperparameter tuning is always required.
Early stopping is irreversible
— halting training early prevents overfitting but may leave the model underfit if the patience threshold is too aggressive.
Validation split reduces training data
— holding out data for validation means less data for training, slightly increasing the risk of underfitting, especially for small datasets.
More data is not always feasible
— when labelled data is scarce (common in specialised agentic tasks), data augmentation and strong regularisation become necessary but introduce their own complexity.
How it works
The bias-variance decomposition
For a regression model, the expected prediction error on a new point can be decomposed into three terms:
Bias

is the average systematic error: how far the model's average prediction is from the true value. A high-bias model makes the same type of mistake everywhere because it lacks the capacity to express the true function.
Variance

is the model's sensitivity to the specific training sample: a high-variance model produces very different predictions if trained on a different random draw of the same underlying data. Irreducible noise is the inherent stochasticity in the data-generating process and cannot be reduced by any model.

Increasing model complexity monotonically decreases bias (the model can fit more complex functions) but monotonically increases variance (it becomes more sensitive to the specific training set). This is the core tension. The optimal complexity minimises the sum of squared bias and variance.
Learning curves as a diagnostic
The most reliable diagnostic tool is the learning curve: a plot of training loss and validation loss across training epochs or across increasing dataset sizes.
Overfitting signature
: training loss continues to decrease while validation loss begins rising. The curves diverge after an early-stopping threshold.
Underfitting signature
: both curves plateau at a poor loss value early in training and fail to improve. The gap between training and validation loss is small (both are high).
Optimal signature
: training and validation loss both decrease and converge, with a small and stable gap.
Regularisation mechanics
Regularisation techniques add a penalty to the loss function that discourages large or complex parameter values, reducing variance at the cost of a slight increase in bias.
L2 regularisation (Ridge)
adds a penalty proportional to the sum of squared weights:
L1 regularisation (Lasso)
adds a penalty proportional to the sum of absolute weights, which encourages sparsity:

In neural networks, dropout randomly zeroes a fraction of activations during each forward pass. This prevents neurons from co-adapting — relying on specific other neurons — which reduces variance. The effective ensemble interpretation: dropout samples a different sub-network at each step, and inference averages across them implicitly.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the canonical bias-variance trade-off curve. The x-axis represents increasing model complexity (deeper network, higher polynomial degree, more trees). The blue curve is training error — it falls monotonically. The pink curve is validation error — it forms a U-shape with a minimum at the optimal complexity. Three zones are marked: underfitting (high bias, both errors high), optimal, and overfitting (low training error, rising validation error).

attrs.alt: Bias-variance trade-off curve showing training error and validation error versus model complexity, with underfitting, optimal, and overfitting zones marked

content.text: The vertical dashed line marks the optimal complexity: the point at which the sum of squared bias and variance is minimised. All practical model selection strategies — cross-validation, early stopping, regularisation — are attempts to find this line without knowing it in advance.
When to use it
Apply overfitting controls when
The model has many more parameters than training examples — classic overfit regime.
Training accuracy is significantly higher than validation accuracy (gap > 5–10 percentage points).
Validation loss begins rising while training loss continues to fall during training.
The model produces overconfident, brittle outputs on inputs slightly outside the training distribution.
Fine-tuning an LLM on fewer than a few thousand examples.
Apply underfitting controls when
Both training and validation accuracy are low and plateau early.
The model converges quickly (within a few epochs) but at a poor loss value.
A baseline rule-based system outperforms the trained model.
Adding regularisation or reducing model size made things worse.
When neither is the issue

If training and validation loss are both low and close, and the model still underperforms, the problem is likely distribution shift (train and test distributions differ), label noise, or missing features — not the bias-variance trade-off. Overfitting and underfitting diagnoses assume that the train and test sets are drawn from the same distribution.
How to implement
Step 1 — Establish evaluation splits

Never evaluate a model's generalisation on data used for training. A minimum setup is an 80/20 train-validation split. For hyperparameter tuning, a three-way split (train / val / test) prevents the test set from influencing model selection.

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# 70% train, 15% validation, 15% test
X_trainval, X_test, y_trainval, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.15, random_state=42
)
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    X_trainval, y_trainval, test_size=0.176, random_state=42  # 0.176 of 85% ≈ 15%
)
print(f"Train: {len(X_train)}, Val: {len(X_val)}, Test: {len(X_test)}")
Step 2 — Implement early stopping
Early stopping monitors validation loss and halts training when it stops improving. This is the most effective single technique against overfitting in neural networks and gradient boosting.

import numpy as np

class EarlyStopping:
    """Halt training when validation loss stops improving."""

    def __init__(self, patience: int = 10, min_delta: float = 1e-4):
        self.patience = patience
        self.min_delta = min_delta
        self.best_loss = np.inf
        self.counter = 0

    def step(self, val_loss: float) -> bool:
        """Return True if training should stop."""
        if val_loss < self.best_loss - self.min_delta:
            self.best_loss = val_loss
            self.counter = 0
        else:
            self.counter += 1
        return self.counter >= self.patience


# Usage in a training loop (pseudocode):
# early_stop = EarlyStopping(patience=15)
# for epoch in range(max_epochs):
#     train_loss = train_one_epoch(model, train_loader, optimizer)
#     val_loss   = evaluate(model, val_loader)
#     if early_stop.step(val_loss):
#         print(f"Stopping at epoch {epoch}")
#         break
Step 3 — Choose and tune regularisation
Start with L2 regularisation (weight decay) as the default. Tune the strength using logarithmic search across a validation set before grid-searching other hyperparameters.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

alphas = np.logspace(-3, 3, 13)  # 0.001 to 1000
results = []

for alpha in alphas:
    model = Ridge(alpha=alpha)
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    train_mse = mean_squared_error(y_train, model.predict(X_train))
    val_mse   = mean_squared_error(y_val,   model.predict(X_val))
    results.append((alpha, train_mse, val_mse))
    print(f"alpha={alpha:8.3f}  train={train_mse:.4f}  val={val_mse:.4f}")

best_alpha = min(results, key=lambda r: r[2])[0]
print(f"\nBest alpha: {best_alpha}")
Step 4 — Add dropout for neural networks

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class RegularisedNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim: int, hidden_dim: int, output_dim: int,
                 dropout_p: float = 0.3):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(p=dropout_p),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(p=dropout_p),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, output_dim),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

# Dropout is automatically disabled during model.eval() — no extra code needed.
model = RegularisedNet(input_dim=10, hidden_dim=64, output_dim=1, dropout_p=0.4)
Examples
Example 1 — Polynomial regression: all three regimes

This runnable example fits polynomial regression at three degrees and prints the train/test MSE, demonstrating underfitting (degree 1), good fit (degree 4), and overfitting (degree 15) on the same dataset.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

np.random.seed(0)
X = np.sort(np.random.uniform(0, 10, 80)).reshape(-1, 1)
y = np.sin(X).ravel() + np.random.normal(0, 0.25, 80)

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.25, random_state=0
)

print(f"{'Degree':>8} {'Train MSE':>12} {'Test MSE':>12} {'Diagnosis':>20}")
print("-" * 56)
for degree in [1, 4, 15]:
    poly = PolynomialFeatures(degree=degree, include_bias=False)
    model = LinearRegression()
    model.fit(poly.fit_transform(X_train), y_train)
    train_mse = mean_squared_error(y_train, model.predict(poly.transform(X_train)))
    test_mse  = mean_squared_error(y_test,  model.predict(poly.transform(X_test)))
    gap = test_mse - train_mse
    if gap > 0.5:
        diagnosis = "Overfitting"
    elif train_mse > 0.3:
        diagnosis = "Underfitting"
    else:
        diagnosis = "Good fit"
    print(f"{degree:>8} {train_mse:>12.4f} {test_mse:>12.4f} {diagnosis:>20}")

# Expected output:
#  Degree    Train MSE     Test MSE           Diagnosis
# --------------------------------------------------------
#       1       0.3841       0.4295         Underfitting
#       4       0.0596       0.0801             Good fit
#      15       0.0141       1.8443          Overfitting
Example 2 — L2 regularisation rescues the degree-15 model

This example applies Ridge regression to the same degree-15 polynomial features, showing that L2 regularisation dramatically reduces validation error while barely affecting training performance for moderate alpha values.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

# (Reuse X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test from Example 1)

poly = PolynomialFeatures(degree=15, include_bias=False)
X_train_p = poly.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_p  = poly.transform(X_test)

print(f"{'Alpha':>10} {'Train MSE':>12} {'Test MSE':>12} {'Note':>25}")
print("-" * 63)
for alpha in [0, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0, 100.0]:
    model = Ridge(alpha=alpha)
    model.fit(X_train_p, y_train)
    tr  = mean_squared_error(y_train, model.predict(X_train_p))
    tst = mean_squared_error(y_test,  model.predict(X_test_p))
    note = ""
    if alpha == 0:
        note = "← unregularised, overfits"
    elif tst < 0.15:
        note = "← sweet spot"
    print(f"{alpha:>10} {tr:>12.4f} {tst:>12.4f} {note:>25}")

# The test MSE drops from ~1.84 (alpha=0) to ~0.08 (alpha≈1) confirming
# that regularisation recovers the generalisation the unregularised model lost.
Real-world example
Fine-tuning an intent classifier for an agentic customer-support system

A support agent pipeline routes incoming messages to tools (order tracking, refund initiation, FAQ lookup) based on classified intent. The team fine-tunes a small transformer-based classifier on 1,200 labelled support tickets.
After the first fine-tuning run they observe 97% training accuracy and 61% validation accuracy — a 36-point gap, a clear overfitting signal. The following changes are applied:
Early stopping
with patience=5 epochs on validation F1 — reduces training from 30 epochs to 9.
Weight decay
of 1e-2 added to the AdamW optimiser.
Data augmentation
via back-translation (English → Spanish → English) doubles the labelled set to 2,400 examples.
Lower learning rate
(2e-5 → 5e-6) on the transformer backbone to preserve pre-trained representations.

from transformers import AutoModelForSequenceClassification, TrainingArguments, Trainer
from datasets import Dataset

# Minimal training config showing the anti-overfitting choices
training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./intent-classifier",
    num_train_epochs=30,                # max, early stopping will halt sooner
    per_device_train_batch_size=16,
    per_device_eval_batch_size=32,
    learning_rate=5e-6,                 # low LR to avoid large weight updates
    weight_decay=0.01,                  # L2 regularisation on all non-bias params
    evaluation_strategy="epoch",
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
    metric_for_best_model="eval_f1",
    greater_is_better=True,
    save_strategy="epoch",
    save_total_limit=2,
    # patience=5 is handled via EarlyStoppingCallback (see transformers docs)
)

# After retraining:
# Train F1: 0.94   Val F1: 0.89   (gap closed from 36 points to 5 points)
After retraining with these controls, the validation F1 rose from 0.61 to 0.89 and the train-val gap narrowed from 36 to 5 points. Live A/B testing confirmed a 28% reduction in misrouted tickets.
Common mistakes
Evaluating on training data
: the single most common error. Any metric computed on the data the model was optimised on is an optimistic estimate of generalisation. Always hold out a validation set before touching the optimiser.
Treating high training accuracy as success
: 99% training accuracy is consistent with severe underfitting (if the task is easy) or severe overfitting (if the task is hard). Neither case indicates a useful model.
Data leakage

: preprocessing steps like standardisation, PCA, or vocabulary construction must be fit on training data only, then applied to validation and test. Fitting on the full dataset leaks validation statistics into training, producing an artificially low validation error.
Applying regularisation before diagnosing
: if the model is already underfitting, adding dropout or weight decay will worsen performance. Diagnose first, then choose the remedy.
Using a single train/test split for hyperparameter search

: if the same validation set is used to select every hyperparameter, it effectively becomes a training set for model selection. Use cross-validation or a separate held-out test set that is never touched during selection.
Stopping based on training loss alone
: training loss decreases monotonically during gradient descent. The optimal stopping point is defined by validation loss, not training loss.
Ignoring class imbalance when reading accuracy
: 99% accuracy on a 99:1 imbalanced dataset is achieved by predicting the majority class always — a model that has learned nothing. Use F1, AUROC, or precision-recall curves.
Best practices
Plot learning curves for every training run
. A visual of training vs. validation loss over epochs is the primary diagnostic instrument.
Start simple
. Begin with a low-capacity model and increase complexity only when validation performance plateaus and training performance is still improving.
Use cross-validation
for datasets smaller than ~10,000 examples. k-fold cross-validation gives a lower-variance estimate of generalisation than a single split.
Standardise preprocessing on training data only
. Fit scalers, tokenisers, and encoders on X_train, then call transform (not fit_transform) on X_val and X_test.
Tune regularisation strength with logarithmic search
. Lambda values of 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 cover five orders of magnitude and usually bracket the optimum.
Set early-stopping patience relative to learning rate schedule
. With cosine annealing or warm restarts, validation loss temporarily worsens during warm restarts — set patience long enough to survive a cycle.
For LLM fine-tuning, monitor perplexity on a separate held-out set
and stop before the model's general question-answering capability degrades.
Keep the test set sealed
until the final model is chosen. Every peek at test metrics during hyperparameter tuning inflates your estimate of generalisation.
Performance & scalability
Computational cost of validation

Running validation after every epoch adds a constant multiple to training time (typically 10–30% overhead, depending on dataset size and the train/val split ratio). This cost is almost always justified: without validation metrics, the training run cannot be stopped at the right time.
Cross-validation cost

k-fold cross-validation multiplies training cost by k. For expensive models (large neural networks, gradient-boosted ensembles on large datasets), full cross-validation is often impractical. Alternatives:
Stratified hold-out
: a single split with careful stratification by class and time (for time-series). Fast but higher variance.
5-fold CV on a subset
: run cross-validation on 20% of the data to tune hyperparameters, then retrain on the full training set with the selected configuration.
Bayesian hyperparameter optimisation
(e.g. Optuna, Hyperopt): requires fewer validation runs than grid search by learning a surrogate model of the validation surface.
Overfitting at scale

Very large models (transformers with billions of parameters) overfit differently. With enough data, high-capacity models achieve both low bias and low variance — modern LLMs exhibit a 'double descent' phenomenon where error decreases again after the classical interpolation threshold. In practice, pre-trained LLMs are rarely overfit during fine-tuning because fine-tuning updates only a small fraction of total parameters, and the pre-trained weights act as strong regularisation.
Memory during training

Storing validation metrics and best-checkpoint weights requires minimal memory. Early stopping implementations that save the best model checkpoint require approximately one extra copy of the model weights on disk, not in GPU memory.
Tooling & ecosystem
scikit-learn

Provides out-of-the-box cross-validation, learning curve plotting, and regularised estimators (Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet). The learning_curve utility is particularly useful for diagnosing whether overfitting or underfitting is active.

from sklearn.model_selection import learning_curve
from sklearn.svm import SVR
import numpy as np

train_sizes, train_scores, val_scores = learning_curve(
    SVR(kernel="rbf", C=1.0),
    X, y,
    train_sizes=np.linspace(0.1, 1.0, 10),
    cv=5,
    scoring="neg_mean_squared_error",
    n_jobs=-1,
)
# train_scores and val_scores are arrays of shape (n_train_sizes, n_cv_folds)
# Mean and std across folds:
train_mean = -train_scores.mean(axis=1)
val_mean   = -val_scores.mean(axis=1)
# Plot train_mean and val_mean vs train_sizes to diagnose bias/variance.
PyTorch / Lightning
PyTorchLightning's Trainer includes built-in early stopping and checkpoint callbacks. Weight decay is passed directly to optimiser constructors.

import pytorch_lightning as pl
from pytorch_lightning.callbacks import EarlyStopping, ModelCheckpoint

trainer = pl.Trainer(
    max_epochs=100,
    callbacks=[
        EarlyStopping(monitor="val_loss", patience=10, mode="min"),
        ModelCheckpoint(monitor="val_loss", save_top_k=1, mode="min"),
    ],
    log_every_n_steps=1,
)
Weights & Biases (wandb)

Logs training and validation metrics per step, making learning curves available in the dashboard with no extra code. Sweeps automate hyperparameter search with Bayesian optimisation, reducing the number of training runs needed to find the optimal regularisation strength.
Optuna

A hyperparameter optimisation library that prunes unpromising trials early (using successive halving or asynchronous successive halving), allowing efficient regularisation search across many orders of magnitude with a fraction of the compute of grid search.
Comparison with alternatives
Several modelling strategies and their relationship to the overfitting-underfitting spectrum:
Strategy
Addresses
Mechanism
Key limitation
L2 regularisation (Ridge)
Overfitting
Penalises large weights
Does not induce sparsity
L1 regularisation (Lasso)
Overfitting + feature selection
Penalises absolute weight magnitude, zeroes irrelevant features
Can be unstable with correlated features
Dropout
Overfitting (neural nets)
Random neuron deactivation during training
Adds training noise; requires careful rate tuning
Early stopping
Overfitting
Halts training at minimal validation loss
Requires validation set; incompatible with some LR schedules
Data augmentation
Overfitting
Increases effective dataset size with synthetic variations
Augmentations must be semantics-preserving
Ensemble methods (RF, XGBoost)
Both
Combine multiple weak learners; bagging reduces variance, boosting reduces bias
Higher inference cost; less interpretable
Increasing model capacity
Underfitting
More parameters can represent complex functions
Risk of overfitting without corresponding data increase
Feature engineering
Underfitting
Adds informative inputs the model lacked
Requires domain expertise; manual effort
Security & reliability
Overfitting and adversarial robustness

A model that overfits memorises quirks of the training distribution. Adversarial inputs — small perturbations crafted to fool the model — exploit exactly these quirks. Regularised models that generalise better are empirically more robust to adversarial examples, though dedicated adversarial training (augmenting the training set with adversarial examples) provides stronger guarantees.
Data leakage as a reliability risk

Data leakage — inadvertently including validation or future information in training — creates models that appear to generalise but will fail in production. Common leakage sources include: fitting preprocessing transformers on the full dataset before splitting; including features derived from the target in input features; using future data in time-series forecasting models. Leakage-induced overfitting is particularly dangerous because offline metrics look healthy until the model hits production.
Train-test distribution shift

Even a well-regularised model will overfit to the training distribution if that distribution differs from the deployment distribution. Practices that improve robustness include: distributional testing (sliced evaluation on demographic or temporal subgroups), continuous monitoring of prediction distributions in production, and scheduled retraining when distributional drift is detected.
Model cards and documentation

Documenting the train-val split strategy, regularisation choices, and the train-val gap at final model selection is essential for production reliability. When a model is retrained on new data, these records allow auditors to detect whether overfitting controls were applied consistently.
Mental model

Imagine fitting a smooth curve through a scatter of noisy data points on a graph. Underfitting is using a ruler to draw a straight line through data that clearly follows a sinusoidal curve — the model is too rigid to follow the actual shape. Overfitting is using a very flexible rubber band that passes through every individual point, including all the noise, forming wild oscillations between observations — it fits the data you have but will miss any new points.

The right model is a smoothly fitted curve that follows the general shape of the data while ignoring point-to-point jitter. Regularisation is the mechanism that makes the rubber band slightly stiffer. Early stopping is the decision to stop bending the rubber band the moment it starts fitting the noise rather than the signal.
Key takeaways
Overfitting
is low training error and high validation error — the model learned noise rather than signal.
Underfitting
is high error on both training and validation — the model lacks the capacity or training needed to capture the real pattern.
The bias-variance trade-off
formalises this: increasing model complexity reduces bias but raises variance; the optimal point minimises their sum.
Learning curves — training and validation loss plotted over epochs — are the primary diagnostic tool and should be examined for every training run.
Regularisation
(L1, L2, dropout) and
early stopping
are the primary remedies for overfitting; increasing capacity, adding features, or reducing regularisation addresses underfitting.
Data leakage — fitting preprocessing on the full dataset before splitting — is the most dangerous source of spuriously good validation metrics.
LLM fine-tuning is especially prone to overfitting on small supervised datasets; use low learning rates, weight decay, and validation perplexity monitoring to keep generalisation intact.
For production systems, maintain a sealed test set, document regularisation choices, and monitor prediction distributions continuously to detect distributional shift after deployment.
